content:
# Numbers Chapter 6 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 153-154 | Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0115-0116 (LXX)
**Source Images:** `NUM_006_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_006_page_002.jpg`; `NUM_006_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_006_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**2** "Speak to the Sons of Israel and say to them: When a man or a woman makes a special vow, the vow of a Nazirite, to separate himself to YHWH,

**3** he shall separate himself from wine and strong drink. He shall not drink vinegar of wine or vinegar of strong drink, and he shall not drink any juice of grapes, and he shall not eat grapes, fresh or dried.

**4** All the days of his separation he shall not eat anything that is produced from the grapevine, from the seeds even to the skin.

**5** All the days of the vow of his separation no razor shall pass over his head. Until the days are fulfilled for which he separates himself to YHWH, he shall be holy; he shall let the locks of the hair of his head grow long.

**6** All the days of his separation to YHWH he shall not come near to a dead person.

**7** For his father or for his mother, for his brother or for his sister -- he shall not make himself unclean for them when they die, for the consecration of his God is upon his head.

**8** All the days of his separation he is holy to YHWH.

**9** And if anyone dies very suddenly beside him, and he defiles the head of his consecration, then he shall shave his head on the day of his cleansing; on the seventh day he shall shave it.

**10** And on the eighth day he shall bring two turtledoves or two young pigeons to the priest, to the entrance of the tent of meeting.

**11** And the priest shall offer one as a sin offering and the other as a burnt offering, and shall make atonement for him, because he sinned on account of the dead person. And he shall consecrate his head on that day.

**12** And he shall separate to YHWH the days of his separation, and shall bring a male lamb in its first year as a guilt offering. But the former days shall fall away, for his separation was defiled.

**13** And this is the law of the Nazirite: on the day when the days of his separation are fulfilled, he shall be brought to the entrance of the tent of meeting,

**14** and he shall present his offering to YHWH: one male lamb in its first year without blemish as a burnt offering, and one ewe lamb in its first year without blemish as a sin offering, and one ram without blemish as a peace offering,

**15** and a basket of unleavened bread, cakes of fine flour mixed with oil, and unleavened wafers anointed with oil, and their grain offering and their drink offerings.

**16** And the priest shall bring them before YHWH and shall offer his sin offering and his burnt offering.

**17** And the ram he shall offer as a sacrifice of peace offerings to YHWH, together with the basket of unleavened bread. And the priest shall offer its grain offering and its drink offering.

**18** And the Nazirite shall shave the head of his separation at the entrance of the tent of meeting, and shall take the hair of the head of his separation and put it on the fire that is under the sacrifice of the peace offerings.

**19** And the priest shall take the boiled shoulder of the ram, and one unleavened cake from the basket, and one unleavened wafer, and shall put them upon the hands of the Nazirite after he has shaved the head of his separation.

**20** And the priest shall wave them as a wave offering before YHWH. It is holy for the priest, together with the breast of the wave offering and the thigh of the contribution. And after that the Nazirite may drink wine.

**21** This is the law of the Nazirite who vows his offering to YHWH for his separation, besides what his hand can attain. According to the vow which he vows, so he shall do, according to the law of his separation."

---

**22** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**23** "Speak to Aaron and to his sons, saying, 'Thus you shall bless the Sons of Israel, saying to them:

**24** YHWH bless you and keep you;

**25** YHWH make His face shine upon you and be gracious to you;

**26** YHWH lift up His face upon you and give you peace.'

**27** And they shall put My name upon the Sons of Israel, and I Myself will bless them."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Notes
1. **v.2 -- "makes a special vow"** (Heb. *yaphli' lindor*, יַפְלִא לִנְדֹּר): The verb *phala'* in the Hiphil means "to do something extraordinary, to make special." The vow of the Nazirite is thus marked from the outset as an exceptional, voluntarily intensified act of dedication. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders *megalōs euxētai euchēn*, "greatly vows a vow," capturing the intensifying force.
2. **v.2 -- "Nazirite"** (Heb. *nazir*, נָזִיר): From the root *nzr*, "to separate, to consecrate." The term denotes one who is set apart by a vow of consecration to YHWH. The LXX transliterates as a verbal form, *hagnisthēnai hagneian* ("to purify a purification"), interpreting the Nazirite state as ritual purity rather than mere separation.
3. **v.3 -- "strong drink"** (Heb. *shekhar*, שֵׁכָר): Any fermented beverage other than grape wine, including date wine, beer, and other grain- or fruit-based intoxicants. The LXX renders *sikera*, a direct transliteration of the Semitic term, which passed into Greek usage.
4. **v.4 -- "from the seeds even to the skin"** (Heb. *mechartsannim ve'ad-zag*, מֵחַרְצַנִּים וְעַד־זָג): The *chartsannim* are the grape seeds (kernels) and the *zag* is the grape skin (husk). The Talmud (Nazir 34b) debates which term refers to which part. Our rendering follows the majority view and the apparent etymology. The LXX reads *apo stemphulon heōs gigartou*, reversing the order, with *stemphula* ("pressed grape skins") and *gigarton* ("grape seed").
5. **v.5 -- "locks"** (Heb. *pera'*, פֶּרַע): The uncut, freely growing hair. The same root appears in the "loosening" of the suspected adulteress's hair in Num 5:18. For the Nazirite, the unshorn hair is the visible sign of the vow, analogous to the high priest's consecration (cf. Lev 21:10-12, which uses the same vocabulary of *nezer* and *pera'*).
6. **v.7 -- "consecration of his God is upon his head"** (Heb. *ki nezer Elohav al rosho*, כִּי נֵזֶר אֱלֹהָיו עַל־רֹאשׁוֹ): The word *nezer* ("consecration, crown") is cognate with *nazir*. The unshorn hair functions as a visible crown of dedication, paralleling the *nezer* (diadem) worn by the high priest (Exod 29:6). The LXX renders *euchē theou autou epi kephalēs autou*, "the vow of his God is upon his head."
7. **v.9 -- "very suddenly"** (Heb. *befeta' pit'om*, בְּפֶתַע פִּתְאֹם): A doubled expression of suddenness, lit. "in a sudden, suddenly." The pairing of near-synonyms emphasizes that the defilement was involuntary and unforeseen, which distinguishes this case from deliberate contact with the dead. The LXX renders *exapina*, "suddenly," collapsing the doublet.
8. **v.12 -- "the former days shall fall away"** (Heb. *vehayyamim harishonim yippelu*, וְהַיָּמִים הָרִאשֹׁנִים יִפְּלוּ): Lit. "the first days shall fall." The metaphor of days "falling" (being voided, annulled) is striking. All previously accumulated days of separation are lost; the count restarts entirely. The LXX reads *hai hēmerai hai proterai alogoi esontai*, "the former days shall be without account."
9. **v.13 -- "he shall be brought"** (Heb. *yavi' oto*, יָבִיא אֹתוֹ): The Hebrew is ambiguous -- it can mean "one shall bring him" (impersonal passive sense) or "he shall bring him [i.e., himself]." Context and the LXX (*prosaxei auton*, "he shall bring him") suggest the Nazirite is formally presented at the sanctuary, possibly escorted.
10. **v.18 -- "the fire that is under the sacrifice of the peace offerings"** (Heb. *al-ha'esh asher tachat zevach hashlamim*, עַל־הָאֵשׁ אֲשֶׁר תַּחַת זֶבַח הַשְּׁלָמִים): The consecrated hair is burned in the fire beneath the peace offering, not simply discarded. This indicates that the hair, as the visible sign of the vow, participates in the sacrificial conclusion of the Nazirite period.
11. **v.19 -- "boiled"** (Heb. *beshelah*, בְּשֵׁלָה): The Qal passive participle of *bashal*, "to cook, to boil." The shoulder (*zeroa'*) of the ram is cooked (not roasted as with the Passover lamb, cf. Exod 12:9), reflecting a distinct ritual protocol. The LXX reads *ton brachiona ton hephthon*, "the boiled arm/shoulder."
12. **v.20 -- "wave offering ... contribution"** (Heb. *tenufah ... terumah*, תְּנוּפָה ... תְּרוּמָה): Two distinct categories of priestly portions. The *tenufah* ("wave offering") involves a horizontal movement before YHWH; the *terumah* ("contribution" or "heave offering") involves a vertical lifting. Both the breast and the thigh go to the officiating priest.

### Structural Notes
13. **Two-part chapter**: Numbers 6 divides into two distinct sections: (a) the law of the Nazirite (vv.1-21) and (b) the priestly blessing (vv.22-27). The juxtaposition is deliberate: the Nazirite law addresses individual consecration to YHWH, while the priestly blessing extends YHWH's consecrating presence to all the Sons of Israel. The movement is from the particular to the universal.
14. **The Nazirite law (vv.1-21)** is structured in three parts: (a) the three prohibitions during the vow -- wine/grape products (vv.3-4), the razor (v.5), and contact with the dead (vv.6-8); (b) the remedy for accidental defilement (vv.9-12); and (c) the completion rites (vv.13-21). The threefold prohibition mirrors the threefold priestly blessing that follows.
15. **The priestly blessing (vv.24-26)** exhibits a carefully ascending structure: three lines of three, five, and seven Hebrew words respectively. Each line begins with the divine name YHWH. The progression moves from material protection (*bless ... keep*), to relational favor (*face shine ... be gracious*), to the fullness of *shalom* (*lift up face ... give peace*). This is one of the oldest texts in the Hebrew Bible, attested on two silver scrolls from Ketef Hinnom (late seventh or early sixth century BCE).
16. **v.27 -- "I Myself will bless them"** (Heb. *va'ani avarekhem*, וַאֲנִי אֲבָרְכֵם): The emphatic pronoun *'ani* ("I") places the ultimate agency of blessing with YHWH, not the priests. The priests speak the words; YHWH effects the blessing. This verse functions as a theological safeguard against a merely magical understanding of the priestly formula.

### Cross-References
17. **v.2 -- The Nazirite**: The most prominent Nazirites in Scripture are Samson (Judg 13:5, 7; 16:17), who was consecrated from the womb, and Samuel (1 Sam 1:11), whose mother Hannah dedicated him. The prophet Amos laments that Israel made the Nazirites drink wine (Amos 2:11-12), violating the first prohibition.
18. **v.5 -- Unshorn hair**: The connection between unshorn hair and divine power is dramatized in the Samson narrative (Judg 16:17-19), where the cutting of his hair breaks his Nazirite consecration and the Spirit of YHWH departs from him.
19. **v.7 -- Priority of consecration over mourning**: The Nazirite's prohibition from mourning even for parents parallels the restriction placed on the high priest (Lev 21:11). This elevates the Nazirite to a quasi-priestly status during the period of the vow.
20. **v.14 -- Three offerings**: The burnt offering (*'olah*), sin offering (*chattat*), and peace offering (*shelamim*) together constitute a comprehensive sacrificial sequence: total dedication (burnt), purification (sin), and communion (peace). This same triad appears at the ordination of the Aaronic priests (Lev 8-9).
21. **vv.24-26 -- Ketef Hinnom inscriptions**: Two small silver scrolls (KH1 and KH2), discovered in 1979 in a burial cave near Jerusalem, bear a version of the priestly blessing and are dated to the late pre-exilic or early post-exilic period. They constitute the oldest known texts of the Hebrew Bible and confirm the antiquity of this liturgical formula.
22. **v.26 -- "peace"** (Heb. *shalom*, שָׁלוֹם): The climactic word of the blessing. *Shalom* encompasses not merely the absence of conflict but wholeness, completeness, and well-being in every dimension -- material, relational, and spiritual. The LXX renders *eirēnēn*, "peace."

### LXX Variants (Vaticanus)
23. **v.2 -- "to purify a purification"**: The LXX renders *nazir* not as a title but as a verbal action: *hagnisthēnai hagneian*, "to purify oneself with a purification." This interpretive translation shifts the focus from the status of "Nazirite" to the act of ritual purification, reflecting Hellenistic Jewish understanding of the vow.
24. **v.5 -- "holy"**: The LXX adds *hagios estai* ("he shall be holy") in agreement with the MT, and renders *pera'* as *komē* ("hair of the head"), a common Greek term for long hair, losing the technical Hebrew nuance.
25. **v.7 -- "consecration"**: Where the MT reads *nezer Elohav* ("the consecration/crown of his God"), the Vaticanus reads *euchē theou autou* ("the vow of his God"), interpreting the *nezer* as the vow itself rather than as a crown or diadem upon the head.
26. **v.9 -- "suddenly"**: The MT's emphatic doublet *befeta' pit'om* is reduced in the LXX to the single adverb *exapina* ("suddenly"), losing the rhetorical force of the Hebrew pairing.
27. **v.12 -- "the former days shall be without account"**: The LXX renders *yippelu* ("shall fall") as *alogoi esontai* ("shall be without reckoning"), a more interpretive rendering that makes explicit the legal meaning: the days are annulled and do not count toward fulfillment of the vow.
28. **v.18 -- "upon the fire"**: The LXX reads *epi to pur to hupo tēn thusian tou sōtēriou*, "upon the fire that is under the sacrifice of the salvation-offering," using *sōtērion* for Hebrew *shelamim*. The rendering *sōtērion* ("salvation-offering") interprets the peace offering through the lens of deliverance rather than wholeness.
29. **vv.24-26 -- The priestly blessing in the LXX**: The Vaticanus preserves a close rendering: *Eulogēsai se Kurios kai phulaxai se; epiphanai Kurios to prosōpon autou epi se kai eleēsai se; eparai Kurios to prosōpon autou epi se kai dōē soi eirēnēn.* Notable: the LXX uses *Kurios* ("Lord") for YHWH throughout, following standard Septuagint convention. The verb *eleēsai* ("have mercy") for Hebrew *vichunnekka* ("be gracious to you") introduces a slightly different theological nuance -- mercy (compassion on the undeserving) rather than grace (unmerited favor).
30. **v.27 -- "I will bless them"**: The LXX reads *kagō eulogēsō autous*, "and I will bless them," preserving the emphatic first-person pronoun (*kagō* = *kai egō*, "and I myself"), matching the Hebrew *va'ani*.
